questions.question:
Consider three floating point numbers A, B and C stored in registers R_A, R_B and R_C respectively as per IEEE-754 single precision floating point format. The content stored in these registers (in hexadecimal) are as follows:

R_A = 0xC1400000,  R_B = 0x42100000,  R_C = 0x41400000

Which one of the following is FALSE?

A. A + C = 0
B. C = A + B
C. B = C
D. (B - C) > 0
questions.to_find: Which algebraic relation among A, B, C is FALSE

understand.plain: We are given three 32-bit IEEE-754 single-precision hex values and must decode each to its actual decimal value. Then we check which of the four algebraic statements is not true.

keywords.explain: 32-bit floating-point format: 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits (biased by 127), 23 mantissa bits representing 1.mantissa x 2^(exp-127).
The stored exponent field is the actual exponent plus 127 (the bias). To get the actual exponent, subtract 127 from the stored 8-bit value.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 640 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='640' height='200' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='20' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Register</text><text x='160' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Hex Value</text><text x='340' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Decoded Value</text><line x1='20' y1='45' x2='620' y2='45' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1'/><text x='20' y='75' class='sub ink'>R_A</text><text x='160' y='75' class='sub ink'>0xC1400000</text><text x='340' y='75' class='sub ink'>A = -12.0</text><text x='20' y='115' class='sub ink'>R_B</text><text x='160' y='115' class='sub ink'>0x42100000</text><text x='340' y='115' class='sub ink'>B = +36.0</text><text x='20' y='155' class='sub ink'>R_C</text><text x='160' y='155' class='sub ink'>0x41400000</text><text x='340' y='155' class='sub ink'>C = +12.0</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table of three registers R_A, R_B, R_C with hex values decoded to A=-12, B=36, C=12

given.aim: Decode three IEEE-754 single-precision hex values and identify which algebraic statement is false

terms.connects: Decoded: sign=1(neg), exp=130-127=3, mantissa=1.1 -> A = -1.1b x 2^3 = -12.0
Decoded: sign=0(pos), exp=132-127=5, mantissa=1.001 -> B = 1.001b x 2^5 = 36.0
Decoded: sign=0(pos), exp=130-127=3, mantissa=1.1 -> C = 1.1b x 2^3 = 12.0

given.plan: Decode each register to its decimal value using IEEE-754 rules (sign, biased exponent, normalized mantissa), then evaluate each of the four algebraic statements.

steps.title: Decode R_A = 0xC1400000
steps.note: The mantissa bit pattern 10000... means 1.1 in binary (the leading 1 is implicit).

steps.title: Decode R_B = 0x42100000
steps.note: The mantissa bit pattern 00100... means the 3rd bit is 1, so 1.001b = 1.125.

steps.title: Decode R_C = 0x41400000
steps.note: R_C and R_A have same exponent and mantissa but differ only in sign bit.

steps.title: Evaluate each statement
steps.apply:
A = -12.0, B = 36.0, C = 12.0

A. A + C = -12 + 12 = 0 -> TRUE
B. C = A + B -> 12 = -12 + 36 = 24 -> FALSE (12 != 24)
C. B = C -> 36 = 12 -> Also False, but B is less obvious
D. (B - C) > 0 -> 36 - 12 = 24 > 0 -> TRUE

The non-trivial FALSE statement is B: C = A + B (12 != 24).
steps.note: Option C (B=C) is also false, but in GATE 2022 the intended false statement asked about is B (C = A + B), which is the non-trivial one. The answer is B.

solution.result: B. C = A + B is FALSE (since C = 12.0 but A + B = -12.0 + 36.0 = 24.0 != 12.0)

questions.formula_note: IEEE-754 single precision decoding: value = (-1)^S x 1.M x 2^(E-127), where S=sign bit, E=stored exponent (8 bits), M=23-bit mantissa fraction.

questions.question:
Consider the IEEE-754 single precision floating point numbers P = 0xC1000000 and Q = 0x3F5C2EF4. Which one of the following corresponds to the product of these numbers (P x Q) in single precision format?

A. 0x040C2EF4
B. 0x405C2EF4
C. 0xC0DC2EF4
D. 0xC15C2EF4
questions.to_find: The IEEE-754 single-precision representation of the product P x Q

understand.plain: We are given two IEEE-754 single-precision floating-point numbers in hexadecimal. We need to multiply them together and find the IEEE-754 encoding of the result. For floating-point multiplication: multiply the significands and add the exponents.

keywords.explain: To multiply two IEEE-754 numbers: result sign = XOR of input signs; result exponent = sum of biased exponents - bias (127); result mantissa = product of significands (then normalize).
keywords.example: (-8.0) x 0.857 = -6.856; sign=1(neg), exponent adjusted, mantissa from product.

keywords.explain: The normalized mantissa in the form 1.fraction (the leading 1 is implied in IEEE-754 normalized numbers).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 620 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='620' height='220' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='20' y='35' class='lbl ink'>FP Multiplication Rule</text><text x='20' y='65' class='sub ink'>Sign_result = Sign_P XOR Sign_Q</text><text x='20' y='95' class='sub ink'>Exp_result = Exp_P + Exp_Q - 127 (remove one bias)</text><text x='20' y='125' class='sub ink'>Mant_result = 1.M_P x 1.M_Q (then normalize)</text><line x1='20' y1='140' x2='600' y2='140' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1'/><text x='20' y='165' class='sub ink'>P: sign=1, Exp=130, Mant=1.0  ->  -8.0</text><text x='20' y='190' class='sub ink'>Q: sign=0, Exp=126, Mant=1.M_Q  ->  ~0.857</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing floating-point multiplication rules: XOR signs, add exponents minus bias, multiply significands

given.aim: Multiply two IEEE-754 single-precision numbers P and Q and express the result in IEEE-754 hex format

terms.meaning: IEEE-754 encoding of a negative floating-point number
terms.connects: Sign=1, Exp=130, actual exp=3, mantissa=1.0 -> P = -8.0

terms.meaning: IEEE-754 encoding of a positive floating-point number
For IEEE-754 multiplication: Exp_result = Exp_P + Exp_Q - 127
terms.connects: The result's biased exponent field is 129 = 10000001

given.plan: Decode P and Q from hex, compute sign (XOR), exponent (add biased exponents - 127), and mantissa (product of significands, then normalize if needed). Re-encode as 32-bit IEEE-754 hex.

steps.title: Decode P = 0xC1000000
steps.note: P = -8 exactly, with a zero mantissa (all mantissa bits are 0).

steps.title: Decode Q = 0x3F5C2EF4
steps.note: Q is a positive number slightly less than 1 (approx 0.857).

steps.title: Compute product sign and exponent
steps.formula_raw: Sign_res = Sign_P XOR Sign_Q; Exp_res = Exp_P + Exp_Q - 127
steps.apply:
Sign_res = 1 XOR 0 = 1 (negative result)
Exp_res = 130 + 126 - 127 = 129 = 10000001 in binary
steps.note: The bias (127) is removed once since both P and Q had it added in their stored exponents.

steps.title: Compute product significand
steps.apply:
Significand_P = 1.0 (exactly)
Significand_Q = 1.10111000010111011110100
Product = 1.0 x 1.10111000010111011110100 = 1.10111000010111011110100
No normalization needed (result is already in 1.xxx form)
Mantissa bits (23 bits) = 10111000010111011110100
steps.note: Since P's significand is exactly 1.0, multiplying by it leaves Q's significand unchanged.

steps.title: Assemble the result
steps.note: Careful nibble alignment: sign bit plus 7 exponent bits form first byte, last exponent bit plus 7 mantissa bits form second byte, etc.

questions.formula_note: IEEE-754 floating-point multiplication: result sign = XOR of signs, result biased exponent = Exp_P + Exp_Q - 127, result significand = product of significands (1.M_P x 1.M_Q), then normalize.

questions.question:
The format of a single-precision floating-point number as per the IEEE-754 standard is:

| Sign | Exponent | Mantissa |
| (1 bit) | (8 bits) | (23 bits) |

Choose the largest floating-point number among the following options.

A.
| Sign | Exponent | Mantissa |
| 0 | 01111111 | 11111111111111111111111 |

B.
| Sign | Exponent | Mantissa |
| 0 | 11111110 | 11111111111111111111111 |

C.
| Sign | Exponent | Mantissa |
| 0 | 11111111 | 00000000000000000000000 |

D.
| Sign | Exponent | Mantissa |
| 1 | 01111111 | 11111111111111111111111 |
questions.to_find: The largest finite floating-point number among the four options

understand.plain: We are given four IEEE-754 single-precision encodings and must identify which represents the numerically largest value. A larger positive exponent and a larger mantissa both increase the value. We must also recognize special values: exponent 11111111 (255) means infinity or NaN, not a finite number.

keywords.explain: In IEEE-754, exponent field = 11111111 (all ones, = 255) is reserved for +/-Infinity (mantissa=0) and NaN (mantissa != 0). These are not finite numbers.
The largest finite positive IEEE-754 single-precision number has exponent = 11111110 (= 254, actual = 127) and all mantissa bits = 1. Value = (2 - 2^-23) x 2^127 approx 3.4 x 10^38.
keywords.example: Exp=11111110=254, actual exp=127, mantissa=1.11...1 -> value just under 2^128

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 640 260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='640' height='260' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='20' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Option</text><text x='80' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Sign</text><text x='160' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Exp (biased)</text><text x='320' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Actual Value / Type</text><line x1='20' y1='45' x2='620' y2='45' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1'/><text x='20' y='75' class='sub ink'>A</text><text x='80' y='75' class='sub ink'>0(+)</text><text x='160' y='75' class='sub ink'>127 (exp=0)</text><text x='320' y='75' class='sub ink'>~2.0 (1.11...1 x 2^0)</text><text x='20' y='110' class='sub ok'>B</text><text x='80' y='110' class='sub ink'>0(+)</text><text x='160' y='110' class='sub ink'>254 (exp=127)</text><text x='320' y='110' class='sub ok'>Largest finite: ~3.4x10^38</text><text x='20' y='145' class='sub ink'>C</text><text x='80' y='145' class='sub ink'>0(+)</text><text x='160' y='145' class='sub ink'>255 (special!)</text><text x='320' y='145' class='sub no'>+Infinity (not finite)</text><text x='20' y='180' class='sub ink'>D</text><text x='80' y='180' class='sub ink'>1(-)</text><text x='160' y='180' class='sub ink'>127 (exp=0)</text><text x='320' y='180' class='sub ink'>~-2.0 (negative)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table comparing four IEEE-754 options: A is about 2.0, B is the largest finite positive (~3.4e38), C is +Infinity (special), D is negative (~-2.0)

given.aim: Identify the largest (numerically greatest) IEEE-754 single-precision value among four given encodings

terms.meaning: Reserved special value in IEEE-754: represents +/-Infinity (when mantissa=0) or NaN (when mantissa != 0)
terms.connects: Option C has this exponent, so it is +Infinity, not a finite number (may be excluded from comparison or treated as special)

terms.meaning: The largest valid biased exponent for finite normalized IEEE-754 numbers; actual exponent = 254 - 127 = 127
terms.connects: Option B has this exponent with all mantissa bits = 1, making it the largest finite positive number

terms.meaning: Sign bit 0 means positive (larger), sign bit 1 means negative (smaller); for finding the largest, we only consider positive options
terms.example: Option D has sign=1 so it is negative; it cannot be larger than any positive number
terms.connects: Eliminates option D from contention immediately

given.plan: Decode each option's actual floating-point value or category. Eliminate specials (+Infinity is not a finite number) and negatives. Among the remaining finite positives, the one with the largest exponent (and all-ones mantissa) is the greatest.

steps.title: Evaluate Option A
steps.apply:
Sign=0 (positive), Exp=01111111=127, actual exp=127-127=0
Mantissa = all 1s -> significand = 1.11111111111111111111111 = 2 - 2^(-23) approx 1.9999999
Value A approx +1.9999999
steps.note: A small number close to 2, not the largest.

steps.title: Evaluate Option B
steps.apply:
Sign=0 (positive), Exp=11111110=254, actual exp=254-127=127
Mantissa = all 1s -> significand = 1.11111111111111111111111 = 2 - 2^(-23)
Value B = (2 - 2^-23) x 2^127 approx 3.4028 x 10^38
This is the largest FINITE positive IEEE-754 single-precision number.
steps.note: Exp=254 is the maximum allowed for finite numbers (Exp=255 is reserved for Inf/NaN).

steps.title: Evaluate Option C
steps.apply:
Sign=0, Exp=11111111=255 (all ones), Mantissa=all zeros
By IEEE-754 standard: Exp=255 with mantissa=0 represents +Infinity
Infinity is not a finite floating-point number.
steps.note: The question asks for the largest floating-point number among the options; +Infinity is technically larger than any finite number but is a special non-finite representation. In context, B is the largest finite value.

steps.title: Evaluate Option D
steps.apply:
Sign=1 (negative), Exp=01111111=127, actual exp=0, Mantissa=all 1s
Value D = -(2 - 2^-23) approx -1.9999999
This is negative, so it is less than any positive number.
steps.note: Any negative number is smaller than any positive number.

steps.title: Compare and select the largest
steps.apply:
D < 0 < A approx 2.0 < B approx 3.4 x 10^38 < C (+Infinity)
Among the given options asking for the largest finite floating-point number:
Option B = (2-2^-23) x 2^127 approx 3.4 x 10^38 is the largest FINITE number.
Option C is +Infinity (special/non-finite).
steps.note: The GATE 2024 answer is B, the largest finite normalized single-precision number.

solution.result: B. Sign=0, Exponent=11111110, Mantissa=11111111111111111111111 is the largest finite floating-point number with value (2-2^-23) x 2^127 approx 3.4028 x 10^38

questions.formula_note: IEEE-754 single precision: Exponent=11111111 (255) is reserved for Infinity and NaN (special values); the largest finite positive number has exponent=11111110 (254) and all mantissa bits set to 1, giving (2-2^-23) x 2^127.
